questions.question: What is LINQ in C#? What are the two syntax styles?
questions.answer:
LINQ (Language Integrated Query) is a .NET feature that brings query capabilities directly into C#. It provides a uniform syntax to query any data source that implements IEnumerable<T> (in-memory collections) or IQueryable<T> (databases via EF Core, XML, etc.).

Two syntax styles — both compile to identical IL:

Query Syntax (SQL-like)

var result = from emp in employees
             where emp.Salary > 50000
             orderby emp.Name ascending
             select new { emp.Name, emp.Department };

// With join
var query = from emp in employees
            join dept in departments on emp.DeptId equals dept.Id
            where emp.Salary > 50000
            select new { emp.Name, DeptName = dept.Name };

Method Syntax (lambda chains — more common in practice)

var result = employees
    .Where(emp => emp.Salary > 50000)
    .OrderBy(emp => emp.Name)
    .Select(emp => new { emp.Name, emp.Department });

// With join
var query = employees
    .Join(departments,
          emp  => emp.DeptId,
          dept => dept.Id,
          (emp, dept) => new { emp.Name, DeptName = dept.Name })
    .Where(x => x.Name != null);

LINQ data source types:

  • LINQ to Objects — queries in-memory IEnumerable<T> collections. All C# is valid.
  • LINQ to Entities (EF Core) — queries IQueryable<T>, translates to SQL. Only operations the provider can translate work.
  • LINQ to XML — queries XDocument/XElement objects.
  • LINQ to DataSet — queries ADO.NET DataSets.

followUps.question: When would you prefer query syntax over method syntax?
followUps.answer:
Query syntax tends to be more readable for complex multi-table joins and grouping operations where the SQL-like structure is clearer:

// Query syntax — cleaner for complex joins + grouping
var query = from o in orders
            join c in customers on o.CustomerId equals c.Id
            join p in products  on o.ProductId  equals p.Id
            group o by c.Name into g
            select new { Customer = g.Key, OrderCount = g.Count() };

// Method syntax equivalent — harder to read with multiple joins
var query = orders
    .Join(customers, o => o.CustomerId, c => c.Id, (o, c) => new { o, c })
    .Join(products,  x => x.o.ProductId, p => p.Id, (x, p) => new { x.o, x.c, p })
    .GroupBy(x => x.c.Name)
    .Select(g => new { Customer = g.Key, OrderCount = g.Count() });

In practice, most developers use method syntax for simple queries and query syntax for complex joins. There's no performance difference — both compile to the same code.

questions.tip: LINQ to Objects vs LINQ to Entities: LINQ to Objects runs all logic in C# memory. LINQ to Entities translates to SQL — only SQL-translatable operations work. If you call a custom C# method inside a LINQ to Entities query, it throws at runtime unless you first call .AsEnumerable() to switch to client-side evaluation.

questions.question: What is deferred execution vs immediate execution in LINQ?
questions.answer:
This is one of the most important LINQ concepts for writing correct, performant code.

Deferred Execution

A LINQ query is defined when you write it, but not executed until you actually iterate over the results. The query object is just a description of what to do — not the result.

Operators that defer: Where, Select, OrderBy, GroupBy, Join, Take, Skip, SelectMany

var employees = new List<Employee> { ... };

// QUERY IS DEFINED — no execution yet
var query = employees.Where(e => e.Salary > 50000);

// Modify the source after defining the query
employees.Add(new Employee { Name = "Alice", Salary = 80000 });

// QUERY EXECUTES NOW (foreach triggers execution)
foreach (var emp in query) // Alice will be included!
    Console.WriteLine(emp.Name);

// Each iteration re-executes the query
foreach (var emp in query) // executes again from scratch
    Console.WriteLine(emp.Name);

Immediate Execution

These operators force the query to execute right now and materialise the results into memory.

Operators that execute immediately: ToList(), ToArray(), ToDictionary(), Count(), First()/FirstOrDefault(), Single(), Any(), All(), Sum(), Max(), Min(), Average()

// ToList() executes immediately — results are fixed at this point
var results = employees.Where(e => e.Salary > 50000).ToList();

// Adding to source now has no effect on 'results'
employees.Add(new Employee { Name = "Alice", Salary = 80000 });
// results still does not contain Alice

Practical impact:

  • In EF Core: deferred queries build up SQL and execute in one round-trip when materialised. Adding .Where() after another .Where() combines into one SQL query.
  • Call .ToList() when you want to snapshot results and prevent repeated database calls.
  • Don't call .ToList() prematurely in EF Core — it loads all data to memory before filtering.

followUps.question: What is the danger of deferred execution with EF Core?
followUps.answer:
The classic mistake: calling ToList() too early, then filtering in memory:

// BAD — loads ALL employees from DB, then filters in C# memory
var result = context.Employees.ToList()
    .Where(e => e.DepartmentId == 5);
// SELECT * FROM Employees  (loads 1M rows!)

// GOOD — filter runs in SQL, only matching rows transferred
var result = context.Employees
    .Where(e => e.DepartmentId == 5)
    .ToList();
// SELECT * FROM Employees WHERE DepartmentId = 5 (fast!)

Also: N+1 problem — querying a list then accessing a navigation property in a loop triggers a separate query per row. Fix with .Include() to eager-load related data.

questions.tip: Rule: Keep LINQ queries deferred (IQueryable) as long as possible in EF Core to let the DB do the filtering. Only call ToList()/ToArray() when you need the data in memory.

questions.question: What are the most commonly used LINQ methods? Give examples of each.
questions.answer:
Here are the LINQ methods you'll use daily:

Filtering

var adults   = people.Where(p => p.Age >= 18);
var first    = people.FirstOrDefault(p => p.Name == "Alice"); // null if not found
var single   = people.SingleOrDefault(p => p.Id == 5);        // throws if >1 match

Projection (transformation)

var names = people.Select(p => p.Name);
var dtos  = people.Select(p => new PersonDto { Id = p.Id, FullName = p.Name });

// SelectMany — flatten nested collections
var allTags = posts.SelectMany(p => p.Tags); // each post has a list of tags

Ordering

var sorted    = people.OrderBy(p => p.LastName).ThenBy(p => p.FirstName);
var descSorted = people.OrderByDescending(p => p.Age);

Aggregation

int    count   = employees.Count(e => e.DeptId == 3);
decimal total  = orders.Sum(o => o.Amount);
decimal avg    = employees.Average(e => e.Salary);
decimal max    = employees.Max(e => e.Salary);
decimal min    = employees.Min(e => e.Salary);

Paging

int page = 2, pageSize = 10;
var paged = products.Skip((page - 1) * pageSize).Take(pageSize);

Set operations

var unique   = list1.Distinct();
var combined = list1.Union(list2);     // distinct union
var both     = list1.Intersect(list2); // items in both
var diff     = list1.Except(list2);    // in list1 but not list2

Quantifiers

bool hasAdults = people.Any(p => p.Age >= 18);  // true if any match
bool allAdult  = people.All(p => p.Age >= 18);  // true if all match
bool hasAlice  = people.Any(p => p.Name == "Alice");

Grouping

var byDept = employees
    .GroupBy(e => e.DepartmentId)
    .Select(g => new { DeptId = g.Key, Count = g.Count(), Avg = g.Average(e => e.Salary) });

followUps.question: What is the difference between First() and Single()?
followUps.answer:
First() returns the first matching element. It doesn't care if there are multiple matches. Single() asserts there is exactly one match — it throws InvalidOperationException if zero or more than one element matches.

  • First() — use when you want the top result and duplicates are expected/acceptable.
  • Single() — use when you know (and enforce) uniqueness, e.g., looking up by primary key.
  • FirstOrDefault() / SingleOrDefault() — same but return null (or default value) instead of throwing when no match is found.

With LINQ to Entities: Single() generates SELECT TOP 2 SQL to check for duplicates. First() generates SELECT TOP 1. For primary key lookups where you know it's unique, FirstOrDefault() is slightly more efficient than SingleOrDefault().

questions.tip: Null safety in C# 8+: FirstOrDefault() can return null for reference types. In nullable contexts, the return type is T? — handle the null case explicitly to avoid NullReferenceException.

questions.question: How do you implement a LEFT JOIN in LINQ? What is the difference from an INNER JOIN?
questions.answer:
LINQ's Join() method performs an INNER JOIN — only elements with matching keys on both sides are included. For a LEFT JOIN (include all left elements, null for non-matching right), use GroupJoin() combined with SelectMany() and DefaultIfEmpty().

INNER JOIN (Join)

// Method syntax
var innerJoin = employees
    .Join(departments,
          emp  => emp.DepartmentId,     // key selector from left (employees)
          dept => dept.Id,              // key selector from right (departments)
          (emp, dept) => new            // result selector
          {
              emp.Name,
              DeptName = dept.Name
          });
// Only employees who have a matching department are included

LEFT JOIN (GroupJoin + SelectMany + DefaultIfEmpty)

// Method syntax
var leftJoin = employees
    .GroupJoin(
        departments,
        emp  => emp.DepartmentId,
        dept => dept.Id,
        (emp, depts) => new { emp, depts })
    .SelectMany(
        x => x.depts.DefaultIfEmpty(), // DefaultIfEmpty() = null if no matching dept
        (x, dept) => new
        {
            x.emp.Name,
            DeptName = dept?.Name ?? "No Department" // null-safe
        });

// Query syntax (easier to read for left join)
var leftJoin = from emp in employees
               join dept in departments
                   on emp.DepartmentId equals dept.Id
                   into deptGroup          // group join
               from dept in deptGroup.DefaultIfEmpty() // flatten with default
               select new
               {
                   emp.Name,
                   DeptName = dept == null ? "No Department" : dept.Name
               };

followUps.question: When should you use LINQ joins vs navigation properties in EF Core?
followUps.answer:
In EF Core, if you have proper navigation properties defined, you rarely need explicit LINQ joins. EF Core translates navigation property access into the appropriate JOINs automatically:

// Navigation property — no explicit join needed
var query = context.Employees
    .Include(e => e.Department)  // LEFT JOIN Departments ON ...
    .Where(e => e.Department.Name == "Engineering")
    .Select(e => new { e.Name, e.Department.Name });

// Explicit LINQ join — needed when no navigation property exists
// or when joining on non-FK columns
var query = context.Employees
    .Join(context.Departments, e => e.DeptCode, d => d.Code,
          (e, d) => new { e.Name, d.Name });

Prefer navigation properties — they're cleaner and EF Core generates efficient SQL. Use explicit LINQ joins for non-standard join conditions.

questions.tip: Query syntax for left joins: The query syntax version with 'into' and 'DefaultIfEmpty()' is significantly more readable than the method syntax. This is one case where query syntax wins.

questions.question: How does GroupBy work in LINQ? Give a practical example with multiple aggregates.
questions.answer:
GroupBy groups elements by a key and returns an IEnumerable<IGrouping<TKey, TElement>>. Each group has a Key property and is itself an enumerable of the elements in that group.

Basic GroupBy:

var byDept = employees.GroupBy(e => e.DepartmentId);

foreach (var group in byDept)
{
    Console.WriteLine($"Dept {group.Key}: {group.Count()} employees");
    foreach (var emp in group)
        Console.WriteLine($"  - {emp.Name}");
}

With Select to project into a summary:

var deptSummary = employees
    .GroupBy(e => e.DepartmentId)
    .Select(g => new
    {
        DepartmentId = g.Key,
        HeadCount    = g.Count(),
        AvgSalary    = g.Average(e => e.Salary),
        MaxSalary    = g.Max(e => e.Salary),
        MinSalary    = g.Min(e => e.Salary),
        TotalPayroll = g.Sum(e => e.Salary),
        Names        = g.Select(e => e.Name).ToList()
    })
    .OrderByDescending(x => x.HeadCount);

Group by multiple keys (anonymous type):

var byDeptAndYear = employees
    .GroupBy(e => new { e.DepartmentId, Year = e.HireDate.Year })
    .Select(g => new
    {
        g.Key.DepartmentId,
        g.Key.Year,
        Count = g.Count()
    });

Equivalent SQL:

SELECT DepartmentId, COUNT(*) AS HeadCount,
       AVG(Salary), MAX(Salary), MIN(Salary), SUM(Salary)
FROM Employees
GROUP BY DepartmentId
ORDER BY HeadCount DESC;

followUps.question: How does ToLookup differ from GroupBy?
followUps.answer:
Both group elements by a key, but they have different execution models:

  • GroupBy — deferred execution. Returns an IEnumerable of groups. The grouping happens when you iterate.
  • ToLookup() — immediate execution. Creates a Lookup<TKey, TElement> in memory — a dictionary-like structure you can index into directly. Best when you need to look up groups repeatedly by key.
// ToLookup — immediate, use when you need random access by key
var lookup = employees.ToLookup(e => e.DepartmentId);

var engineeringTeam = lookup[3]; // O(1) access by key — no re-enumeration
var hrTeam = lookup[5];

// GroupBy — deferred, use when streaming/building SQL queries
var groups = employees.GroupBy(e => e.DepartmentId); // no execution yet

questions.tip: GroupBy returns groups, not a flat list. Always follow GroupBy with .Select() to project the groups into summary objects. Forgetting .Select() and trying to use the raw IGrouping is a common beginner mistake.

questions.question: What is the difference between LINQ to Objects and LINQ to Entities? What operations are not translatable?
questions.answer:
This distinction is critical for EF Core developers — mixing them up causes runtime exceptions or silent performance disasters.

LINQ to Objects

  • Operates on in-memory IEnumerable<T> collections.
  • Any valid C# code can appear in a lambda — custom methods, string operations, complex logic.
  • Executes entirely in the .NET runtime.
  • No translation — all logic runs in C#.

LINQ to Entities (EF Core)

  • Operates on IQueryable<T> from EF Core's DbSet<T>.
  • EF Core's LINQ provider translates the expression tree to SQL.
  • Only operations the SQL provider can translate work. Custom C# methods cannot be translated.

Common non-translatable operations:

// THROWS at runtime — EF Core can't translate a custom C# method
var result = context.Products
    .Where(p => MyCustomValidation(p.Name)) // can't translate!
    .ToList();

// FIX — move to client-side after initial query
var result = context.Products
    .Where(p => p.IsActive) // translatable — stays SQL
    .AsEnumerable()          // switch to LINQ to Objects
    .Where(p => MyCustomValidation(p.Name)) // runs in C# memory
    .ToList();

// Other non-translatable examples:
.Where(p => p.Name.Contains(someVar, StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase)) // comparison overload not translatable
.Where(p => new List<int> { 1,2,3 }.Contains(p.Id)) // this IS translatable (generates IN clause)

// Translatable operations:
.Where(p => p.Name.StartsWith("A"))     // translates to LIKE 'A%'
.Where(p => p.Name.ToLower() == "test") // translates to LOWER() = 'test'
.Where(p => EF.Functions.Like(p.Name, "%test%")) // explicit LIKE

followUps.question: What is AsEnumerable() vs AsQueryable() and when do you use each?
followUps.answer:
.AsEnumerable() — switches from IQueryable (database) to IEnumerable (client-side). All subsequent LINQ operations run in C# memory. Use it when you need to apply operations EF Core can't translate to SQL. Warning: Everything before .AsEnumerable() runs in SQL; everything after runs in memory. Call it as late as possible to minimise data transferred from the DB.

.AsQueryable() — wraps an IEnumerable as IQueryable so you can compose it with methods that accept IQueryable. Useful when building dynamic query predicates. Does NOT send queries to a database; if the source is already in-memory, the 'queries' still run in-memory.

questions.tip: Golden rule for EF Core LINQ: Keep everything as IQueryable (avoid AsEnumerable) until you've applied all filters, projections, and ordering. The DB should do the heavy lifting. Only materialise (ToList/AsEnumerable) when you need C#-only operations.

questions.question: How do you use Aggregate() in LINQ? What is it equivalent to?
questions.answer:
Aggregate() is LINQ's general-purpose fold/reduce operation. It applies a function to an accumulator and each element, returning a single accumulated result. It's equivalent to Array.reduce() in JavaScript or functools.reduce() in Python.

// Sum using Aggregate (equivalent to .Sum())
int total = numbers.Aggregate(0, (acc, n) => acc + n);

// Product of all numbers
int product = numbers.Aggregate(1, (acc, n) => acc * n);

// String join using Aggregate (equivalent to string.Join(",", ...))
string csv = words.Aggregate((acc, word) => acc + ", " + word);
// More efficient: string.Join(", ", words)

// Find the longest string
string longest = words.Aggregate("", (acc, w) => w.Length > acc.Length ? w : acc);

// Build a complex object
var summary = employees.Aggregate(
    new { Total = 0m, Count = 0 },
    (acc, emp) => new { Total = acc.Total + emp.Salary, Count = acc.Count + 1 },
    acc => new { AvgSalary = acc.Total / acc.Count, acc.Count });

Three Aggregate overloads:

  1. Aggregate(func) — uses first element as seed, throws on empty sequence.
  2. Aggregate(seed, func) — uses provided seed value, safe on empty sequence.
  3. Aggregate(seed, func, resultSelector) — additionally transforms the final accumulated value.

In practice, prefer the built-in aggregates (Sum, Max, Count, Average) for simple operations — they're more readable and EF Core can translate them to SQL. Use Aggregate for custom fold logic that the built-ins can't express.

followUps.question: What is Zip() in LINQ?
followUps.answer:
Zip() merges two sequences element by element, pairing elements at matching positions:

var names  = new[] { "Alice", "Bob", "Charlie" };
var scores = new[] { 95, 87, 92 };

var results = names.Zip(scores, (name, score) => $"{name}: {score}");
// Alice: 95
// Bob: 87
// Charlie: 92

// C# 8+ Tuple overload
var pairs = names.Zip(scores); // IEnumerable<(string, int)>
foreach (var (name, score) in pairs)
    Console.WriteLine($"{name}: {score}");

The result length is the minimum of both sequences — if one sequence is shorter, the extra elements from the longer sequence are ignored.

questions.tip: Prefer readable aggregates: Use Sum(), Max(), Count() etc. instead of Aggregate() where possible — they're clearer and EF Core translates them to SQL. Use Aggregate() when you need custom folding logic that built-ins can't handle.

questions.question: What is the difference between Select() and SelectMany() in LINQ?
questions.answer:
This question trips up a lot of mid-level developers. The difference is about flattening nested sequences.

Select() projects each element to a new form — it's a 1-to-1 transformation. Input: N elements → Output: N elements.

SelectMany() projects each element to a sequence, then flattens all those sequences into one — it's a 1-to-many flattening. Input: N elements → Output: sum of all inner sequence lengths.

var customers = new[]
{
    new { Name = "Alice", Orders = new[] { "Laptop", "Phone" } },
    new { Name = "Bob",   Orders = new[] { "Tablet" } },
    new { Name = "Carol", Orders = new[] { "Monitor", "Keyboard", "Mouse" } }
};

// Select — each customer maps to their orders array (gives IEnumerable<string[]>)
var nestedOrders = customers.Select(c => c.Orders);
// Result: [ ["Laptop","Phone"], ["Tablet"], ["Monitor","Keyboard","Mouse"] ]
// 3 elements, each is an array

// SelectMany — flattens all orders into ONE sequence (gives IEnumerable<string>)
var allOrders = customers.SelectMany(c => c.Orders);
// Result: ["Laptop", "Phone", "Tablet", "Monitor", "Keyboard", "Mouse"]
// 6 elements, flat

Console.WriteLine(string.Join(", ", allOrders));

// Real use case: get all tags across all blog posts
var allTags = blogPosts.SelectMany(p => p.Tags).Distinct();

// SelectMany with result selector — pairing parent with each child
var orderDetails = customers.SelectMany(
    c => c.Orders,
    (customer, order) => new { customer.Name, Order = order }
);
// Result: (Alice, Laptop), (Alice, Phone), (Bob, Tablet), ...

// SQL equivalent of SelectMany:
// SELECT c.Name, o.Order FROM Customers c
// CROSS JOIN UNNEST(c.Orders) AS o(Order)

// Common mistake: using Select when you need SelectMany
var wrong = customers.Select(c => c.Orders); // IEnumerable<string[]>
var right = customers.SelectMany(c => c.Orders); // IEnumerable<string>

I use SelectMany whenever I have a collection of collections that I need to flatten — tags, order lines, phone numbers — anything where each parent has a list of children.

followUps.question: How does SelectMany relate to a SQL CROSS JOIN or JOIN with a collection?
When would SelectMany with a result selector be useful?
What is the query syntax equivalent of SelectMany?

questions.tip: Select = 1-to-1 transform (N→N). SelectMany = 1-to-many flatten (N→sum of inner counts). Think of it as 'flatten'. Any time you find yourself doing .Select().SelectMany(x=>x) to flatten — just use SelectMany directly.
